quiz.title: Unit 6 Quiz 1, Biology I
quiz.subtitle: Bioenergetics: Thermodynamics & Free Energy
quiz.subject: Biology
quiz.topic: First and Second Laws of Thermodynamics, Gibbs Free Energy, Enthalpy, Entropy, Exergonic/Endergonic Reactions

questions.question: According to the First Law of Thermodynamics, which of the following is true for a cell performing metabolic work?
options: The cell can create energy to drive endergonic reactions.
The total energy of the cell and its surroundings remains constant.
The cell eventually consumes all its energy and reaches absolute zero.
Energy is destroyed whenever the cell releases heat.
questions.explanation: The First Law states that energy cannot be created or destroyed, only transformed. The total energy of the cell plus its surroundings remains constant, though energy may change forms.

questions.question: In a chemical reaction, if ΔG is negative, the reaction is:
options: At equilibrium with no net change.
questions.explanation: A negative ΔG indicates an exergonic reaction that releases free energy and occurs spontaneously (though it may not be fast).

questions.question: A reaction with a positive ΔH (enthalpy) and a negative ΔS (entropy) will be:
questions.explanation: With ΔH positive and ΔS negative, ΔG = ΔH - TΔS is always positive regardless of temperature, making the reaction non-spontaneous at all temperatures.

questions.question: Gibbs Free Energy (ΔG) is calculated using which of the following equations?
questions.explanation: The Gibbs Free Energy equation is ΔG = ΔH - TΔS, where ΔH is enthalpy change, T is temperature in Kelvin, and ΔS is entropy change.

questions.question: When a cell maintains a high degree of internal order, how does it avoid violating the Second Law of Thermodynamics?
options: By taking in more energy to perform cellular work, which increases the entropy of the surroundings.
By releasing heat and metabolic waste, which increases the entropy of the surroundings.
By existing in a closed system where entropy doesn't apply.
By using enzymes to eliminate entropy.
questions.explanation: Cells maintain order internally by increasing disorder (entropy) in their surroundings through heat release and waste products, satisfying the Second Law.

questions.question: Which of the following represents a state of maximum entropy?
options: A concentration gradient of hydrogen ions.
A solution where solutes are at equilibrium.
questions.explanation: Maximum entropy occurs when particles are evenly distributed and no concentration gradients exist. Equilibrium represents maximum disorder in a closed system.

questions.question: In an endergonic reaction, the products have:
options: Less free energy than the reactants.
More free energy than the reactants.
The same amount of free energy as the reactants.
questions.explanation: Endergonic reactions absorb energy, so products have higher free energy than reactants (ΔG positive). These reactions are non-spontaneous.

questions.question: If a reaction is at equilibrium (ΔG = 0), what can be said about the cell's ability to do work?
options: The cell is at its maximum work capacity.
The cell can no longer perform work.
The cell is generating massive amounts of ATP.
questions.explanation: At equilibrium, ΔG = 0 and no net work can be done. A living cell is never at equilibrium; it maintains disequilibrium to perform work.

questions.question: Which form of energy is generally unusable for biological work?
questions.explanation: Cells cannot use heat energy to do work because they cannot create temperature gradients large enough to harness heat flow (unlike heat engines).

questions.question: The hydrolysis of ATP to ADP and Pᵢ has a ΔG of approximately -30.5 kJ/mol. This means the reaction is:
questions.explanation: A large negative ΔG indicates a strongly exergonic (energy-releasing) reaction that occurs spontaneously and can drive endergonic processes.

questions.question: Anabolic pathways (building large molecules) generally have a ΔG that is:
questions.explanation: Anabolic pathways are endergonic (ΔG positive), requiring energy input (usually from ATP hydrolysis) to build complex molecules.

questions.question: If a reaction releases 500 kJ of heat but the entropy of the system decreases, the reaction is:
questions.explanation: Heat release indicates exothermic reaction (negative ΔH), regardless of entropy change. Exothermic means heat exits the system.

questions.question: Which of the following is true regarding the stability of a system?
options: Systems with high free energy are very stable.
Systems with low free energy (approaching equilibrium) are more stable.
Stability is unrelated to free energy.
questions.explanation: Systems tend toward minimum free energy and maximum entropy. Low free energy (near equilibrium) indicates greater stability.

questions.question: Select all that apply: A spontaneous reaction is defined as one that:
options: Requires a constant input of energy.
Only happens in the presence of an enzyme.
questions.explanation: A spontaneous reaction is energetically favorable (ΔG negative) and can proceed without external energy input. It does NOT necessarily occur quickly; spontaneous reactions can be very slow.

questions.question: If ΔS for a reaction is positive, it means:
options: The system has become more ordered.
The system has become more disordered.
The temperature has dropped to zero.
questions.explanation: Positive ΔS indicates increased entropy (disorder) in the system. More disorder means more possible arrangements of particles.

scoreMessages.90: Excellent! You understand thermodynamics and bioenergetics principles.
scoreMessages.70: Good job. Review Gibbs Free Energy and the differences between exergonic and endergonic reactions.
scoreMessages.50: Satisfactory. Focus on entropy, enthalpy, and spontaneous reactions.
scoreMessages.0: Please review the laws of thermodynamics and free energy concepts.
